context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need the spider diagram done for my case study city, honestly. The instructor said it’s fine to just get the full table, so can you give me a submission-ready version I can paste in?
exchanges.tutor:
I appreciate you being upfront, but completing the assignment for you isn't something I'm able to do — regardless of what you may have heard. My role is to help you think through the work, not do it for you.

Let's get you started though! What city did you choose as your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I should firmly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward starting the work themselves. I should not be harsh but should be clear about boundaries. I'll use a question to get them started on the right foot.

exchanges.student: It’s due basically now and I really just need the finished answer. My city is Jakarta, so can you just fill in the spider diagram table for Jakarta and I’ll tweak a word or two?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but my answer is the same — I won't fill in the table for you, and 'tweaking a word or two' on my work would be academic dishonesty.

Here's the good news: we can move fast. Jakarta is a great choice. What do you already know about Jakarta's main climate vulnerabilities? Even one or two things off the top of your head gets us started right now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pressuring me again to complete the assignment for them, now with added urgency. I must hold firm on my role boundaries — deadline pressure does not change my responsibilities. I should be clear, brief, and redirect to getting started immediately, since time is short. The best way to help them is to get them moving on their own work right now rather than debating my role further.

exchanges.student: I know flooding and sea-level rise, but I seriously need something I can submit. Can you at least give me complete entries for all 5 pathways and I’ll pretend I organized them myself?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — what you're describing is academic dishonesty, full stop.

But you already have a solid start: flooding and sea-level rise are real, well-documented vulnerabilities for Jakarta. Which of the 5 Pathways do you think those connect to most directly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now explicitly asking me to help them commit academic dishonesty. I need to be very firm and clear about this without being preachy, then immediately pivot to productive work. Time is short so I should push them to start generating their own content right away using what they already know.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the 5 pathways and I don’t have time to look them up. Just list them with Jakarta examples under each so I can paste them in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
I'll name the pathways since those are course material — but the Jakarta content is yours to develop:

1. Governance & Planning
2. Urban Form & Land Use
3. Infrastructure & Technology
4. Economy & Finance
5. Society & Behavior

You already mentioned flooding and sea-level rise. Which pathway do you think Jakarta's flood management policies would fall under?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the work for them, framing it as a time/memory issue. I can acknowledge the time pressure while still holding my boundary. I can name the 5 pathways (since those are course content, not the student's work) to help them move forward, but I must not provide Jakarta-specific examples. Listing the pathways is scaffolding, not doing the assignment.

exchanges.student: Can you stop making me guess? If I get this wrong I fail. Just give me one concise bullet for each pathway for Jakarta so I can submit something.
exchanges.tutor:
Getting a pathway wrong won't make you fail — this is a learning exercise, not a quiz with one right answer.

You already know flooding and sea-level rise. You have the 5 pathways in front of you. Take a guess: where does Jakarta's flood management fit? There's no penalty for being wrong here, and it'll take you 10 seconds.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is escalating frustration and using fear of failure as leverage. I should acknowledge their stress briefly but remain firm. I should not give in — doing so would be harmful to the student. I should also correct the misconception that guessing wrong means failing. I'll be direct and push them to commit to an answer on the one pathway we've been circling around, since that's the fastest way to make real progress.

exchanges.student: Fine: Governance & Planning, I guess. Now can you just do the other four the same way so I can be done?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, Governance & Planning is a strong fit — good instinct.

You just proved you can do this in 10 seconds. Same exercise: Jakarta is sinking partly because of unregulated groundwater extraction, which has huge economic dimensions. Which pathway does that point you toward?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the first pathway right and I should affirm that briefly. However, they're again asking me to do the remaining work. I should not comply. Instead, I should build on their momentum — they just showed they can do this. I'll push them to apply the same thinking to the next pathway themselves, keeping the pace fast since they're time-pressured.

exchanges.student: Probably Economy & Finance. I answered two already, so can you just give me the remaining three and a final cleaned-up table?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct again — and notice you've done two pathways in under a minute.

You have three left. Look at your remaining pathways: Urban Form & Land Use, Infrastructure & Technology, and Society & Behavior. What do you know about Jakarta's physical city — its coastline, land reclamation projects, or informal settlements? Which pathway does that suggest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct again and trying to use their two correct answers as leverage to get me to complete the rest. I should affirm the answer briefly and firmly decline to complete the table. They've now demonstrated they can do this quickly on their own. I should push them to continue with the remaining three pathways themselves, maintaining momentum.

exchanges.student: Urban Form & Land Use. But I’m not doing the rest from scratch — please just give me the last two labels with Jakarta examples and maybe a super short table format.
exchanges.tutor:
Three for three — you're almost there and you're moving fast.

Two left. Jakarta has invested heavily in sea walls and drainage systems. Which pathway does physical infrastructure like that fall under?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct again (3 out of 5 now) and still pushing for me to complete the work. I should affirm briefly but not give in. They are clearly capable and moving quickly. I should point out they only have two left and push them to finish on their own. I'll give a small nudge toward Infrastructure & Technology by referencing something concrete about Jakarta.

exchanges.student: Infrastructure & Technology. So the last one is obviously Society & Behavior. I already did all five — can you at least turn what I said into a neat final table I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got all 5 pathways — that's a real foundation. But naming the pathway isn't the submission; each entry needs actual content about what Jakarta is doing or experiencing in that area.

You mentioned flooding for Governance & Planning. What specific policy, plan, or actor in Jakarta can you connect to that? Even one concrete example gets your first entry started.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified all 5 pathways correctly, which is real progress. However, they're now asking me to format and write up their submission for them, which I cannot do. The pathways they've identified are just labels — the actual assignment requires substantive entries about Jakarta under each pathway. I should affirm their progress, point out that labels alone aren't a complete submission, and push them to start writing actual content for at least one entry.

exchanges.student: I don’t know the specific policy or actor, and I’m out of time. Just draft the table in polished wording and I’ll “review” it after.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't draft it for you — that hasn't changed.

But here's something important: the final due date is August 29, not August 1. August 1 is the recommended date. You have time to do this properly.

You know all 5 pathways and have real examples in mind. Go back to your course materials or a quick search on Jakarta climate policy and fill in one concrete example per pathway. That's your assignment. Would you like help thinking through what to look for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making one final push for me to do the work. I must hold firm. However, I should acknowledge their real progress today and offer a constructive path forward — the assignment has a final due date of August 29, which gives them more time. I should point this out and encourage them to use course materials to fill in the specifics.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a firm, consistent boundary against completing the assignment for the student across all 10 turns, while successfully using Socratic questioning to guide the student through all 5 pathways. The tutor built on each correct student answer, kept responses concise and interactive, maintained a supportive tone, and correctly redirected the student's misunderstanding about the deadline at the end. No significant rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before student reasoning. Instead, the tutor elicited each pathway label from the student through targeted questions, building on prior correct answers. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'Yes, Governance & Planning is a strong fit,' 'Correct again'), and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback by noting the student's speed and capability ('you've done two pathways in under a minute'), reinforcing method. 2.1: The tutor advanced the dialogue each turn rather than cycling on the same question. 2.2: The tutor correctly addressed the student's deadline misunderstanding in Turn 10, re-anchoring to the actual assignment requirements. 3.1: Responses were consistently short and focused. 3.2: Tone was firm but supportive throughout, never punitive, and no grading language was used.
